1: šimʿâ YHWH ṣedeq haqšîbâ rinnātî (šimʿâ YHWH ṣedeq haqšîbâ rinnātî): 'Hear a just cause, O LORD; attend to my cry.' Psalm 17 is a ṭĕpillâ ('prayer') — one of only five psalms so labeled (cf. Pss 86, 90, 102, 142). The opening appeal to ṣedeq ('righteousness/justice') frames the prayer as a legal appeal: the psalmist presents his case before the divine judge. The phrase bĕlōʾ śiptê mirmâ, 'from lips free of deceit,' establishes the speaker's credibility — this is not manipulation but genuine appeal. The term rinnâ ('cry, ringing cry') captures the emotional intensity of the plea.
2: millĕpānêkā mišpāṭî yēṣēʾ (millĕp̄āneḵā mišpāṭî yēṣēʾ): 'Let my vindication come from your presence.' The noun mišpāṭ ('judgment, vindication, sentence') is what the psalmist awaits from the divine court. The phrase ʿênêkā tiḥzenâ mêšārîm, 'let your eyes see what is right,' invites YHWH's direct judicial inspection. The use of millĕpānêkā ('from before your face') — divine presence as the source of the verdict — anticipates the beatific conclusion of Psalms 17:15 where the psalmist will see that same face in righteousness.
3: bāḥantā libbî pāqadtā lāylâ (ḇāḥantā libbî pāqaḏtā lāylâ): 'You have tested my heart; you have visited me by night.' The two verbs — bāḥan ('test, examine,' the metallurgical term from Psalms 11:4-5) and pāqad ('visit, muster, examine') — describe thorough divine investigation. The test occurs lāylâ ('by night') — when the psalmist is most vulnerable, most unguarded, most truly himself. The result: ṣĕraptanî bal-timṣāʾ, 'you have refined me; you found nothing.' The psalmist is not claiming sinlessness but innocence in the specific matter of the prayer — his hands are not contaminated by the enemy's violence.
4: lĕpĕʿullôt ʾādām bidbar śĕpātêkā (lipĕʿullôṯ ʾāḏām biḏḇar śĕp̄āṯeḵā): 'Regarding the works of man, by the word of your lips.' The phrase biḏbar śĕp̄āṯeḵā ('by the word of your lips') identifies YHWH's revealed word as the standard of behavior. The clause ʾănî šāmartî ʾōrĕḥôt pārîṣ, 'I have guarded myself from the paths of the violent,' uses pārîṣ ('violent one, destroyer') — the same social category as the enemy psalms describe. The claim is not moral perfectionism but covenant integrity: the psalmist has not followed the way of violence.
5: tāmōk ʾăšûray bĕmaʿglôtêkā (ṯāmōk ʾăšûray bĕmaʿgĕlôṯeḵā): 'My steps have held fast to your paths.' The phrase bal-nāmôṭû pĕʿāmāy, 'my feet have not slipped,' uses the same root môṭ ('to waver, slip') that appears in Psalms 15:5 (the righteous 'shall not be moved') and Psalms 13:4 (the enemy's boast). The psalmist's stability is attributed to divine holding (tāmōk, 'you hold firm') — not self-generated virtue but YHWH's grip on him as he walks in covenant paths.
6: ʾănî qĕrāʾtîkā kî-taʿănēnî ʾēl (ʾănî qĕrāʾṯîḵā ḵî-ṯaʿănēnî ʾēl): 'I call upon you, for you will answer me, O God.' The perfect qĕrāʾtî ('I have called') paired with the imperfect taʿănēnî ('you will answer') creates a confidence structure: past invocation grounds future expectation. The phrase haṭ-ʾoznĕkā lî šmaʿ ʾimrātî, 'incline your ear to me; hear my speech,' uses the anthropomorphism of a king bending close to hear a petition — personal, attentive, relational hearing.
7: haphlēʾ ḥăsādêkā môšîaʿ ḥôsîm (haphlēʾ ḥăsāḏeḵā môšîaʿ ḥôsîm): 'Show your marvelous steadfast love, O Savior of those who take refuge.' The hiphil haphlēʾ ('make wonderful/extraordinary') from pālāʾ ('to be marvelous') calls for ḥesed that exceeds ordinary expectation. The divine title môšîaʿ ḥôsîm, 'savior of those who take refuge,' combines the verb yāšaʿ ('save') with ḥāsâ ('take refuge') — matching the psalm's opening self-description as one who relies on YHWH. The phrase mimmitqômĕmîm bîmînekā, 'from those who rise up against your right hand,' places the enemies in opposition to divine power.
8: šomrēnî kĕʾîšôn bat-ʿāyin (šomrēnî kĕʾîšôn baṯ-ʿāyin): 'Keep me as the apple of the eye.' The phrase ʾîšôn bat-ʿāyin ('apple of the daughter of the eye') — the pupil — is the most tender image of intimate protection: the tiny, precious, reflexively guarded part of the body. The second image, bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā tastîrēnî, 'hide me in the shadow of your wings,' draws on the protective imagery of a mother bird shielding her chicks (Deuteronomy 32:11; Ruth 2:12; Matthew 23:37). Together, the two images — the guarded eye and the sheltering wing — express total, personal, enveloping protection.
9: mippĕnê rĕšāʿîm zû šaddûnî (mippĕnê rĕšāʿîm zû šaddûnî): 'from the wicked who do me violence.' The relative clause zû šaddûnî ('who destroy/deal violently with me') uses the root šādad ('to ruin, deal violently') — a characteristic activity of the wicked in the Psalter. The phrase ʾōyĕbay bĕnepeš yaqqîpû ʿālay, 'my deadly enemies surround me' — literally 'enemies against my soul/life who encircle me' — uses the napšî construction to indicate mortal enmity, not merely personal hostility.
10: ḥelbāmô sāgĕrû pîmô dibbĕrû bĕgēʾût (ḥelbāmô sāgĕrû pîmô dibbĕrû ḇĕḡēʾûṯ): 'They have closed their hearts; with their mouths they speak arrogantly.' The phrase ḥelbāmô sāgĕrû — literally 'their fat they have closed' — uses ḥeleb ('fat') as a metaphor for calloused insensitivity (cf. Psalms 119:70: 'their heart is fat as grease'). The noun gēʾût ('pride, arrogance') describes the content of their speech. The psalmist characterizes the enemy by two failures: a closed heart that cannot receive correction and an arrogant mouth that overreaches all limits.
11: ʾašûrênû ʿattâ sĕbābûnû (ʾašûrênû ʿattâ sĕḇāḇûnû): 'In our steps they now surround us.' The plural surreptitiously shifts from 'me' (the individual psalmist) to 'us' — the community of the righteous who share this threat. The phrase ʿênêhem yāšîtû liṭṭôt bāʾāreṣ, 'they set their eyes to cast down to the ground,' describes the enemy's fixed gaze directed at bringing the righteous to earth — the same spatial language of exaltation and humiliation that runs through Psalms 9-10.
12: dimmyōnô kĕʾaryēh yiksopu litrup (dimmyōnô kĕʾaryēh yiḵsôp liṭrôp): 'He is like a lion eager to tear.' The simile kĕʾaryēh ('like a lion') returns from Psalms 10:9 — the predator imagery for the wicked reaches its intensity here: not merely lurking but eager (yiksopu, 'yearns, longs') for prey. The parallel wĕkĕkĕpîr yōšēb bĕmistārîm, 'like a young lion crouching in ambush,' adds the youth-lion (kĕpîr) — strong and fierce. The two images — mature lion's desire and young lion's ambush — combine appetite with strategy.
13: qûmâ YHWH qaddĕmâh pānāyw (qûmâ YHWH qaḏḏĕmâ p̄ānāyw): 'Arise, O LORD! Confront him, cause him to bow.' The imperative qûmâ ('arise!') is the liturgical summons to divine action (cf. Psalms 9:19; Psalms 10:12). The hiphil hakriʿēhû, 'cause him to bow/subdue him,' requests decisive defeat. The phrase pallĕṭâ napšî mērāšāʿ ḥarbĕkā, 'deliver my soul from the wicked by your sword,' invokes divine weaponry — YHWH himself fighting as a warrior on behalf of the vulnerable. The phrase ḥarbĕkā ('your sword') is an instrument of covenant judgment.
14: mĕmētîm yādĕkā YHWH mĕmētîm miḥeled (mĕmētîm yāḏĕḵā YHWH mĕmētîm miḥeled): 'by your hand, O LORD, from men of the world.' The phrase mĕmētîm ('from the dead / from men who are dying') combined with miḥeled ('from this transient world') categorizes the enemies by their mortality — they belong to the perishable world order. The description of their prosperity — ḥelqām bĕḥayyîm ûṣĕpûnĕkā tĕmallēʾ biṭnām, 'whose portion is in this life and whose belly you fill with your treasure' — does not condemn wealth per se but identifies those whose satisfaction is entirely worldly: they have their reward here (cf. Luke 6:24). The phrase yiśbĕʿû bānîm wĕhinnîḥû yitrām lĕʿôlĕlêhem, 'they are satisfied with children, and they leave their abundance to their infants,' is ironic: the legacy the wicked leave is material, transient, destined to be inherited by those equally vulnerable to mortality.
15: ʾănî bĕṣedeq ʾeḥĕzeh pānêkā (ʾănî bĕṣeḏeq ʾeḥĕzeh p̄āneḵā): 'As for me, I shall behold your face in righteousness.' The emphatic ʾănî ('as for me') makes a final contrast to the worldly satisfied of Psalms 17:14. The verb ḥāzâ ('to behold, see in a vision') carries visionary weight — not a casual glance but a face-to-face encounter. The phrase bĕhāqîṣ ʾeśbaʿ tĕmûnātekā, 'when I awake, I shall be satisfied with your likeness,' introduces the moment of waking — ʾāqîṣ (from qîṣ, 'to awake from sleep'). The word tĕmûnâ ('likeness, form, similitude') — the same word used in Numbers 12:8 for Moses seeing the form of YHWH — is the ultimate beatific vision. Whether the psalmist speaks of morning prayer, resurrection, or eschatological encounter, the hunger of Ps 17 reaches its resolution here: satisfaction comes not from the world's abundance but from the face of God.
